speaker: interviewer
text: July 7, 1995, interview with Peter Malchin, interviewer Sam Kintzer. This interview will be in New York City, New York and will be conducted in English. July 7, 1995, interview with Peter Malchin, interviewer Sam Kintzer. This interview will be conducted in New York City and conducted in English. Mr. Malkin, will Introduce yourself to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter Z. Malchin. That's my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born 1927, May-- May 27. I'm sorry. 27 of May, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1929. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. I went back to Poland and then back to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were born in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. 20 year later, I left Poland with my mother, and we stayed there about six to seven years, and I came back to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is near Lublin in a place called Zolkiewka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zolkiewka. And you went back with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. When you were born in Israel, how many of you were in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Been my brother, my father who died very near to the Second World War, and my mother. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a very fascinating life, and actually a life that-- very interesting incident that happened after the war. You actually, are the man who physically captured Adolf Eichmann. And you have a book written about it. And we want to be able to discuss that in great detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are a painter, and that figures into this as well in great detail. But before we get into that, I would like to talk a little about you, and who you are and your family, and what it was like for you prior to the war. You said you had a-- you had brother at the time when you were born. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yachiel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yachiel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were talking about Poland. In Poland, I had a sister for sure. A sister that I dedicate my book to her is called Fruma. And I had another small brother that been killed. And my sister had three children, and they been-- all-- all my relatives been killed in the Holocaust. So I came-- I came back was my father, mother, and my brother. That's all. From all the families. About 150 relatives died in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Poland when you went over to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember from Poland very, very little. I remember my home that I came back later after-- about four or five years ago. I remember the river. I remember-- one thing that I will never forget, when I was a-- a small boy going from school-- it's called cheder at that time. And it was near the river. And I saw suddenly one time a reflection from the river normally, and it was a big flame in the river, a big light. When I came near to my home, I see that the synagogue in Poland is burning. And the one-- the-- the man who always I bought from him candy in a very small place, a round face I remember, was dead. And the-- the Polish people killed him because already it was kind of feeling of the war is near to Poland. And all these anti-Semites-- anti-Semitic people started to kill a Jew, and the Polish have never been, you know, late in killing Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was not-- it was not new. But to me as a child, that was the first time that I felt it something new to me. And I felt very-- As a small boy, and I remember my mother running, and it was a very big thing in the-- in the small shtetl that they call it. And my mother decided to leave. To leave-- there was a big dispute between my father, that normally the rabbi was the-- the one to  who has to ask if you can move or not. And my mother didn't believe in nobody but her senses. Like, she was really very strong, and she decided we leave-- we leave Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we could never bring back our-- our sister and her three children and my small brother and our relatives. But mostly, it was terrible that my sister has to stay there. And after a year, she decided to come back and to bring my sister, but never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you-- before you left for Israel, at that point, your father-- I'm sorry. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David. And what did he do at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- working, you know, in commerce, wheat. You know, buying from the Polish, bringing into town. That was the most of the work, to bring wheat into-- into the big city from the farm-- farmers. That what he's doing. My mother has a small-- a small grocery, something like this, selling, you know, in the-- in the small shtetl. That's what I remember. I still remember the small place. And when I came back, I remembered my-- the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family religious? Was it a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- religious, you know, religious is many steps. You know, to be fanatic? No. To be-- believe in God? Yes. Even me, I believe in God. I don't know even why. [LAUGHS] I believe, you know, more it's traditional. My mother when she has to cook me something, she didn't mind. You know. [LAUGHS] Exactly how-- how she do it, but she never told my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I was, you know, in the cheder. I liked it. I gave even an-- an example, remember in my book. I said, what's different the difference between the schools? I came later in Israel, about the cheder. In cheder, we sat in a circle, and the rabbi was teaching. And I remember I was almost three years old, I already read-- read the Bible easily.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was in the first grade, because when I came to Israel already, how to say, familiar with the letters and reading. It was-- [INAUDIBLE]. And we'd be sitting, and everybody has a name. You felt, if you are sitting with the-- together like a [INAUDIBLE]. You know. And when I went to the-- to Israel there, to the big school there, I hated the schools because it remind me of factory. Many windows, simple rooms, no atmosphere. And I met a lot of travelers there because I-- I liked much more the cheder. Than the-- and I said, if God has to come to the cheder or to the school, he will easily come to the cheder because only was-- there was only one window. In cheder, was about-- about 200 windows. [LAUGHS] He wouldn't know how to enter to the school. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- in-- in Poland, you went to the cheder. And-- and let me ask about your-- just your family life, in general. What-- what-- what was your relationship like with your-- with your brothers and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was like a-- you know, more than a mother to me than a sister because we had-- there was a big difference in years. And my mother was very occupied. My father was all the time going abroad to bring this wheat and all this. With my brother, how you say, been a very good relationship because he was not only my brother, also my friend. Like if we have to go to swim, we go together. To the forest, we go together. And I have also been small groups. You know. I had one friend of mine, [INAUDIBLE]. It was kind of a small sculpture. And we played mostly in the graveyard. You ask me why. The graveyard was the most silent place, and they had a big space. And I think most-- many Jewish children played in the graveyard because that was the only place that they wouldn't have goyim. They wouldn't have Polish there. And also you have space. And you have hiding places. And I liked it. Even people hate, you know, and they're afraid of graveyard. I still have the atmosphere when I come to a graveyard, the most important thing is how the trees are, how the wind comes, and if it's not too crowded. And I already said to somebody, if you want to put me somewhere after my death, put me in a small place, not in the city because even the graves out there are crowded like crazy. Put me in the place that somebody can read my name. And it shouldn't be fancy and all this. A small place. And that's exactly what was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I visit later this graveyard in Poland. And I found that no grave was there, only one piece of a grave, of a stone there. And-- and I asked a Polish woman there, where are all the Jews here? She said, there'd been 2,000 people. They all died. And I went where my sister-- where my sister lived, not my parents. In Crescent [? Robelsky ?] the name and said, what is in here? It said 5,000 Jews. Nobody stayed alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also the graveyard was nothing in, only a few stones like this. Even the stones they took away to make fancy, because it was marble, so they stole the marble from the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the graveyard when you were playing with your brother, you said it was Yitzach. This was your youngest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitzach. [INAUDIBLE], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had another brother, Jacob?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He died in accident in Israel. So he was a kind of a boy, if you read the book you'll see, that he was not accustomed-- I don't-- I think something happened to my family that it was not like this who died, died, and who stayed alive continued. It was like feeling that we can't tear ourselves away from the Holocaust. All the time talking about my sister and children. My-- My father died because he couldn't believe that he would never see his three grandchilds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how to say, my-- my brother, Jacob, was very attached to my sister. He said, at one time when there was a quarrel, and all this with my brother, he said you know what? I said, and he went to swim, almost drowned himself. There was a big quarrel, and I said to him, why do you do all these things? What happened to you? He said, look, I want to tell you one thing. This world is not accustomed to me, and I am not accustomed to this world. And one day when he walked like this, was an accident. I was walking behind him, and the accident occurred. He went reading. He liked to read a lot. And he read the book, and it came [INAUDIBLE] and killed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw it. And I blame myself a lot. It's only about, I would say about 12 years, I couldn't-- I couldn't-- I couldn't forget the accident. And I blamed myself and my brother that we couldn't bring our Jacob that he will, how do you say, feel that he can live and-- and, you know, and we can live together. I-- I can't-- I was too small to-- but too small to definite what happened really. But I think that the Holocaust, my family lived. And I remember only once-- one time that I said to my mother, I didn't want to hear about the Holocaust. I always ran away because I said, I don't understand it when I hear the word Holocaust. I just want to run away. But one thing I said to my mother, you know, like children talk, I say one day I'm going to kill three Germans for every child of my sister. She said yes, yes. So in a way, it happened. I didn't kill, but one was Eichmann. And I brought more than one Nazi to justice. That's [? not spoken. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you left Poland to go back to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a bit between six and seven. I-- I know that I have to go to the first class, but I never went because I spoke already, can read easily Hebrew. So I didn't. That was my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this came about of your moving to Poland after this incident when the synagogue burnt-- burnt down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother had decided to--

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about? How did your mother get the visas or the necessary papers that she did?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not easy. My father has to-- has to disguise himself as a rabbi because they need in Israel rabbi. And then at that time there was the British. And they didn't let you go so easy. And there was kind of a list, who else will go? And there was differences between Germany and Poland because Germany already was kind of a fear an attack from the Nazi movement and all this. And the Polish, already smell was the atmosphere of the Nazis already. And they-- And they, how do you say, I would say the Polish have a good smell when it comes to anti-Semitism and all this anti-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she has to maneuver a lot. And she-- he became disguised to some profession that's needed here. And me was changed also to Peter because it was [? the other ?] name. Put Peter also. And she brought us-- brought us together, but my sister couldn't go because she had these three children. It was impossible to give a certificate to come to Israel. So she stayed. What was your name? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Fruma.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you-- you said it wasn't Peter. What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have another name. Peter. [? Add to ?] Zvi. Z-V-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zvi. So when you left for Israel, this was before the war, before '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was about-- was not yet war. And we came to Israel. I say, my father start to work in a-- you know, in building houses. Bricks and all this kind of thing. New to us. And I remember we went someplace. It's called [INAUDIBLE]. I remember this hill. And there's no-- no one building. My father said-- We met some relatives, but I didn't like it too much. He want me, you know, a small boy, to babysitting and all of it. He started to talk to me about babysitting. [LAUGHS] My father, I remember, said to my mother, look, I've have never lived in a hill. You know Polish are very flat. I never lived on a hill. Let's go down as I am accustomed. I don't want to stay. In other words, he didn't want to stay there. A very difficult life in the beginning. My-- my brother was very young. He start work and never studied because he have to work to, you know, have some money. So our life was not easy coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who came over? There was-- there was you and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yachiel

speaker: interviewer
text: Yachiel. And Jacob?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob-- and Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jacob.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob died. Yes. As I told you. And Yachiel also died, but about seven years ago. I am the only one who stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so prior to coming and prior before the war, the-- the anti-Semitism in-- in your town, at any rate, was quite-- quite prevalent.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, to-- to live in a Jewish village with the Polish and all this, and I-- when I came back to visit my home and all this, I really felt again the smell of Polish people. [LAUGHS] [? I'm ?] [? not ?] the same for sharing all this. But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the atmosphere of unwanted-- And even now when I came, I felt it now with the Polish because they've been very afraid when I came to see my home that I'm going to take back the home. I said, no. You should know that 200 people, 200 Jews have been killed after the Holocaust when they came back to take their houses, been killed by the Polish when they want to take back their-- their property. So they didn't try anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's say, the atmosphere of unwanted, I felt it very much now, even now. And I felt it then as a boy that you are being afraid when you walk to home with a small lamp, you know, because [INAUDIBLE] with the candle. I've always been afraid of the-- of the Polish. Somebody will attack you. I look like a Polish. I-- when I was big-- bigger, I look more than a-- than English or-- the English looked. And they didn't know. But they always ask me, are you Jewish or English? Now they one day beat me. It's written in the book, I think. They beat me, the English. I still now I don't die for the English because of my-- my beating. [LAUGHS] And that was in Israel?

words.text: It's written in the book, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- I'm sorry to take you back to Poland for a minute. But did you have-- did you have non-Jewish friends at all in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was not an age that you communicate with non-- non-Jewish because normally it was separated. I was in a Jewish school. And I don't think even-- I don't think the Jew ever, only in this small-- in this-- these classes, were together. I think only the higher classes more. It was in the-- in the big cities. They have been together, more-- more together, this Polish and all this. Me? Very little Polish, mostly Hebrew and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Israel and your sister stayed behind and your youngest brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed back.

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- he stayed with her. Did she, at that point, have her own place where she was living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She lived in-- in another town. They moved because she was married to a [? Crosnick ?] [? Robelsky ?]. The name is about-- it was about, let's say, an hour from-- to go with a car, one place to the other place, visit the big church there and all  of this. That I remember well because we lived isolated there a little bit. So she lived there. She has a home and a husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that the youngest son stayed on and didn't go on to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, it was difficult to take him. There was no reason. The second, he was ill. He had something in his throat, and he has to be left there. There's no way that we can get the certificate later or after. It was a hurry. And sometimes I ask myself how my mother did it. You know, it was terrible to think about it. But sometimes in situations, my mother was very strong. My father would never do it. And me, too. When I think about it, I would stay. I say, whatever will happen will happen to all of us. And my mother felt that this is the last moment, did not go out, you will never get the certificate. She was also afraid from the Jewish people. They wouldn't give the certificate because it was a big line to get the certificate. And you should know always, it's like this. That the first who have the good connection, get the certificate. So it was very difficult to get it. And remember at the time of [INAUDIBLE], we go back farther later to Hungary, that only the people get who get the certificate are the relatives [INAUDIBLE]. And some people that been going on, the other people wanted them to go out, and very little who-- who-- who because of other reasons went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's always-- you know, it has some kind of priorities. And my mother was very afraid of priorities. And she knew-- she was very, very clever that even as a child and later, I didn't really-- didn't understand it. But after it, I understood well when I read-- when I read this book of Ben Hecht. Pregidy. Pregidy you say? Perjury.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perjury. And-- and that shows you that it was a big fight, how do you say, to get-- to get-- who will be the first. And my mother understood, if not now, nobody will be safe. I think she was right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived in-- in Israel, you said that you had family there already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There are some family, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live with them at first?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, very bad. I-- [LAUGHS] Normally, when they ask me, my-- my father-- it's a kind of a joke. But it was true. Sometime after the war, long ago, somebody come to him. He wanted to show how he is relative to him. He said, look, if I hate somebody, he's my relative. So don't-- don't make it so-- don't try to show that you are my relative. First, you have to be a mensch, a human being, and right. You don't have to show me that you're a relative. These relatives haven't been, you know, in a way very near to us. And-- and I told you, they want to use me. They want to use my brother. It didn't work. And after two weeks, we left and we went, you know, down from the hill into the valley to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it didn't help.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's when your father went to work in a brick--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brick company. And my mother went to work in the small, how say, sell all the kind of-- I don't how you say [NON-ENGLISH]/ It is a place that you can buy bread, butter, this, all these things. A small-- a small, how do you call it, place that you can buy almost the small things when you need for home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a market?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a market.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the adjustment for you and-- What was the adjustment like for you and your brothers and your family coming from Poland to-- back to Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, you know, you have a lot of fantasy when you studied the Bible. It was the Messiah and the big things when you come to Israel. When I saw the ship coming near to the port, I said to my father, let's go back. He said, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see cars. I didn't-- I see a donkey here, Arabs, all these kind of people. I said, I'm not accustomed to it. I would like to go back. And my father said, don't worry. You never know. You know. He liked the Bible. You will see big things. And how do you say, why do I see big things? I already see what I see and don't like it. Difficult, you know, for people, for children just asked to leave. I remember that when I was separated from this-- from [INAUDIBLE], and I separate from my other friends there, the brother-- the uncle-- my uncle and all this, I said, I remember this small girl. She said to me-- She gave me some handkerchief. And she said, take it and remember me. And I said, why? And she said because we are not going to see each other anymore. And I said, why not? Couldn't believe it. And we had been small children, but still we have already felt the-- the sorry and the grief. We-- we lived it that we are, how do you say, somebody is-- is looking-- want our life. Something is unstable. And mostly we look at-- I [? held ?] the cross between my mother and father, why to leave. I ask my rabbi first. And the rabbi always-- And I have to blame a lot, the rabbis at the time. They say, you have bread? You live. Why do you care? Why do you have to go to Israel and start a new life? And my mother said, I don't want to hear the rabbi. I want to hear everything-- nothing. We leave. So, you know, a lot of people, it was not just like go, leave the country and go, they have to ask their rabbi. They have to ask their authority, like the authority, can we go? What will happen? And they always said, easy, if the God will, if they want. How to say, if God will, you can stay everywhere, anywhere. Why do you have to change your life? You-- you are accustomed. You have your community and all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people listened. A lot of people listened. That's why we have so many in Poland because they're being kind of traditional. And the atmosphere was you have to listen to your rabbi. You don't leave your work. You don't leave the bread you have. You have to try it again. And with God help, everything will be OK. Looks like it was not OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father-- was-- was very close with the rabbi? And was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- There's no one who was not close to the rabbi. You are talking about a small community that everything goes around the synagogue, around the rabbi, and the people knew each other. There wasn't such a thing that you didn't know the one brings the candy, the one with the mail. If you see, how do you call, the you've seen it. Tevye and the seven daughters. That's-- there's no way you can't-- You know everybody. You know who's going to marry. You know the girl. You know this. It was a very familiar with each other. And also, we knew our enemies. They knew whom to talk, whom to give the money to stay alive, to get permits and all this. It was a very close, small community.
